T1 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: C5 innervates the deltoid and biceps, with sensory distribution over the lateral upper arm. C5 radiculopathy causes weakness in shoulder abduction and elbow flexion, not the triceps or wrist extensors. The sensory loss would be proximal, not extending to the hand. Choice B: C6 affects the biceps, brachioradialis, and wrist extensors, and provides sensation to the lateral forearm and thumb/index fingers. C6 involvement may include reduced biceps or brachioradialis reflexes and lateral hand paresthesias, but it spares the middle finger and triceps. Choice D: C8 controls finger flexors and intrinsic hand muscles, with sensory distribution over the medial forearm and fourth/fifth digits. Weakness would be seen in grip strength and finger flexion, not wrist extension or triceps strength. This patient's distribution does not match C8. Choice E: T1 innervates the intrinsic hand muscles, including those responsible for finger abduction and fine motor control. T1 radiculopathy causes sensory loss over the medial arm and may cause difficulty with hand coordination, but not triceps weakness or middle finger numbness. Bottom line: C7 radiculopathy presents with triceps weakness, diminished triceps reflex, wrist extensor weakness, and numbness in the middle finger. It is typically due to foraminal narrowing at the C6–C7 level, and most cases improve with conservative therapy. Strabismus the primary cause of ocular misalignment or diplopia; rather, it is a potential complication of untreated strabismus. Clinically, amblyopia presents as unilateral or bilateral decreased vision without a structural abnormality of the eye, and children typically do not have diplopia since the brain suppresses the weaker image. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying cause (e.g., patching the dominant eye) to force use of the amblyopic eye and stimulate visual development. Choice B: Astigmatism is caused by an irregular curvature of the cornea or lens, resulting in distorted or blurred vision at all distances. The abnormal shape leads to light focusing at multiple points on the retina, compromising image clarity. This condition does not cause eye misalignment, diplopia, or abnormal cover-uncover test findings. Astigmatism commonly presents with visual blurring, headaches, and eyestrain, but does not produce diplopia or strabismus. It is corrected with cylindrical lenses that compensate for the irregular refractive power of the cornea or lens. Choice C: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short anteroposteriorly or the cornea has insufficient curvature, causing light to focus behind the retina. Patients with hyperopia see distant objects more clearly than near objects, often leading to difficulty with close work. Children may compensate with increased accommodation, but this can cause eyestrain or headaches. Hyperopia does not cause ocular misalignment or diplopia, but uncorrected severe hyperopia can predispose to accommodative esotropia, a specific form of strabismus, which may overlap with this presentation but is not the general mechanism described here. Clinical presentation is typically blurry near vision, not double vision or shifting ocular fixation. Choice D: Myopia (nearsightedness) is due to an elongated eyeball or excessive corneal curvature, resulting in light focusing in front of the retina. This causes distant objects to appear blurry while near vision remains clear. Myopia does not cause strabismus or issues with binocular alignment, and patients do not exhibit abnormal findings on cover- uncover testing. Severe myopia increases the risk of retinal detachment due to elongation of the globe, but does not account for the described diplopia or misalignment. Clinical features center around blurry distance vision and frequent squinting, not double vision or depth perception loss. Bottom line: Strabismus is a disorder of ocular alignment that results in binocular vision loss, diplopia, and impaired depth perception, and can cause amblyopia if untreated. Differentiating strabismus from refractive errors like astigmatism, hyperopia, and myopia is critical, as only strabismus presents with eye misalignment and abnormal cover-uncover test. Jaw opening strength Choice E: Tongue protrusion is mediated by the hypoglossal nerve (cranial nerve XII), which exits the skull via the hypoglossal canal. A lesion of CN XII produces tongue deviation toward the side of the lesion due to unopposed action of the contralateral genioglossus muscle. CN XII does not carry taste fibers nor does it innervate facial expression muscles. The absence of tongue deviation and preserved speech makes hypoglossal nerve injury unlikely. This patient’s deficits are best explained by facial nerve dysfunction, not hypoglossal involvement.

Shoulder Dermatomal distribution. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Referred pain to the anterior thigh maps to L2–L4 dermatomes via the femoral nerve, classically seen with hip joint pathology (eg, osteoarthritis, femoral neck fracture), iliopsoas irritation, or L3 radiculopathy. Although proximal ureteral colic originates around T10–T12 and can cause flank/loin pain, it does not typically project to the true anterior thigh distribution of the femoral nerve. In ureteric stones, migration to the distal ureter shifts pain toward L1–L2 dermatomes, favoring the groin rather than the anterior thigh, making this choice incorrect in this clinical setting. Choice C: Medial thigh pain follows the obturator nerve (L2–L4), often implicated in obturator hernias, pelvic sidewall pathology, or hip osteoarthritis causing adductor spasm. This distribution is not the canonical referral pattern for ureteral colic, which tracks sympathetic afferents from T10–L2 and shifts flank → lower quadrant → groin as the stone descends. Because the obturator territory is not the typical target for distal ureteral pain, medial thigh radiation is not the best answer here. Choice D: The perineum is innervated predominantly by the pudendal nerve (S2–S4) and is a hallmark referral zone for anorectal disease, pelvic floor disorders, perineal abscess, and urogenital conditions centered in the distal urethra or external genitalia. Ureteral visceral afferents do not travel with sacral parasympathetics; instead they accompany sympathetic fibers to T10–L2, explaining why perineal radiation is not expected in ureteric stone pain. Given this distinct neuroanatomy, perineal localization is inconsistent with the scenario of migrating ureteral colic. Choice E: The shoulder is a common site of referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation via the phrenic nerve (C3–C5), seen in conditions such as subphrenic abscess, splenic rupture, or peritonitis involving the diaphragm. This dermatomal distribution is entirely unrelated to ureteral innervation, which travels via sympathetic fibers from T10–L2. Because the neuroanatomic pathways are completely different, shoulder radiation would not occur in ureteral colic, making this choice entirely incorrect for this patient’s presentation. Bottom line: Ureteral colic from nephrolithiasis produces severe, colicky flank pain that changes location as the stone migrates, reflecting the T10–L2 visceral afferent pathway. When the stone reaches the distal ureter, pain is referred to the groin via shared innervation with the genitofemoral nerve (L1–L2). Anatomy Neurology (Innervation of Other organ systems) None

Dorsal motor nucleus of the vagus The area postrema, located on the dorsal medulla at the caudal fourth ventricle, is the chemoreceptor trigger zone for vomiting. It lacks a typical blood-brain barrier and detects circulating toxins to initiate the emetic reflex. Lesions to this region abolish vomiting responses without affecting other parasympathetic outputs.

Lesion of the left abducens nerve (CN VI) MLF syndrome is pathognonomic for multiple sclerosis (MS) on USMLE. It will usually be a woman 20s-30s with these eye findings, or MS can present with optic neuritis, which is impaired color vision, impaired visual acuity, or even central scotomas (blind spots). D. Uncal herniation with compression of CN III Wrong answer explanations: Choice B: While this answer describes a plausible mechanism—MLF demyelination—it is incorrect due to lateralization. The patient’s left eye fails to adduct when she looks to the right, which indicates a left-sided MLF lesion. A lesion in the right MLF would impair adduction of the right eye during leftward gaze—a mirror- image presentation. This is a common distractor on exams because students may overlook the importance of gaze direction. Always match the direction of gaze and the affected eye to localize the lesion accurately. Choice C: A lesion of the left CN VI would impair abduction of the left eye, not adduction, and would cause horizontal diplopia when looking to the left. In this vignette, the left eye fails to adduct during rightward gaze, making this diagnosis anatomically incompatible. Furthermore, there would be no nystagmus in the contralateral eye with a CN VI lesion. The patient also lacks other signs of a full horizontal gaze palsy. In cavernous sinus thrombosis, CN VI is often the first and most commonly affected due to its central location within the sinus. It is the most medial nerve in the cavernous sinus, making it vulnerable to compression or inflammation. USMLE likes that. Choice D: A CN III lesion would cause ptosis, pupil dilation, and impaired adduction, elevation, and depression of the eye, often resulting in a “down and out” appearance. None of these signs are present. Convergence is also preserved in this case, which would not be possible with a CN III palsy affecting the medial rectus. The deficit described is specific to horizontal gaze adduction, not global oculomotor dysfunction. Choice E: Uncal herniation occurs when the medial temporal lobe (uncus) is displaced downward through the tentorial notch due to increased intracranial pressure. This compresses the ipsilateral CN III, leading to a dilated pupil, ptosis, and eye positioned "down and out" due to unopposed action of the lateral rectus and superior oblique muscles. Early pupillary dilation is a HY finding on USMLE and indicates brainstem compression. High Yield USMLE Audio Answer: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: Bottom line: MLF syndrome (aka INO) is pathognomonic for MS on USMLE. The patient won't be able to adduct with the ipsilateral eye when abducting the contralateral eye. Convergence is intact, since CN III is intact. Pathology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

While the radial nerve contributes to supination, it does so via the supinator muscle; full supination also requires the biceps, which is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve. The absence of wrist drop and the presence of lateral forearm sensory loss in this patient make radial nerve injury unlikely.

Choice E: The thoracodorsal nerve arises from the posterior cord and innervates the latissimus dorsi muscle, a large muscle involved in arm extension, adduction, and internal rotation. This nerve can indeed be injured during axillary lymph node dissection, as it travels in the axillary region. However, thoracodorsal nerve damage results in weakness with activities like climbing or swimming, not scapular winging. The latissimus dorsi does not stabilize the scapula against the thoracic wall; that role belongs to the serratus anterior. Additionally, this patient has no difficulty with powerful upper limb movements, but instead shows a prominent medial border of the scapula, indicating a serratus anterior issue. Bottom line: The long thoracic nerve innervates the serratus anterior and stabilizes the scapula. Injury leads to scapular winging, especially when pushing against resistance. Surgical trauma, particularly during mastectomy or axillary dissection, is a common cause. Anatomy Neurology (Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles) None

Optic neuritis Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents as sudden, painless, complete monocular vision loss, often associated with a cherry-red spot on fundus exam and a history of vascular risk factors (e.g., atrial fibrillation, carotid atherosclerosis). It does not cause eye pain, afferent pupillary defect, or signs of demyelination elsewhere in the CNS, such as clonus or urinary incontinence. CRAO is an ophthalmologic emergency but is not associated with demyelinating plaques or young women. Choice B: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy affecting the peripheral nervous system, with ascending weakness, areflexia, and possible autonomic instability. GBS does not involve central demyelination, optic neuritis, or upper motor neuron findings. It typically follows a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni) and is managed with IVIG or plasmapheresis. GBS spares the optic nerve, which is part of the CNS. Choice C: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results from demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), also seen in MS. INO is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the contralateral eye during attempted lateral gaze. Unlike optic neuritis, INO does not present with acute vision loss or pain; instead, patients complain of diplopia. Clinical examination shows preserved pupillary light reflex, distinguishing it from optic neuritis. INO is highly associated with MS but is not the best match for the patient's acute monocular visual loss and afferent pupillary defect. Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction, causing fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigability, especially of ocular and bulbar muscles. It causes ptosis and diplopia but does not present with optic nerve dysfunction, afferent pupillary defect, upper motor neuron signs, or MRI findings of white matter plaques. Treatment includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppression, not disease-modifying therapy for demyelination. Bottom line: Optic neuritis is a highly testable initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis, presenting with painful, acute, monocular vision loss and a relative afferent pupillary defect. MS should be considered in young women with CNS demyelination symptoms, including upper motor neuron findings and urinary incontinence. Pathology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion the preferred surgical option, physically separating the offending vessel from the nerve root to relieve compression. Other procedures include percutaneous radiofrequency rhizotomy or gamma knife radiosurgery, which selectively damage pain fibers to reduce symptoms. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Axonal demyelination of facial motor fibers produces an acute, lower motor neuron palsy of cranial nerve VII with forehead involvement, loss of nasolabial fold, impaired eye closure, hyperacusis from stapedius weakness, and possible loss of taste on the anterior two thirds of the tongue. Pathology involves inflammatory demyelination of the facial nerve within the facial canal, leading to conduction block and flaccid facial weakness rather than paroxysmal pain. Symptoms evolve over hours to days and are not characteristically stimulus-triggered. Neurologic examination is abnormal between episodes in true facial nerve palsy. The vignette shows shock-like, touch-precipitated facial pain with a normal exam between attacks, which does not fit facial motor fiber demyelination. Choice B: Compression of the geniculate ganglion affects the sensory and parasympathetic components of the facial nerve, often causing deep otalgia, altered taste, decreased lacrimation, and progressive facial weakness from involvement of adjacent motor fibers. Tumor-related compression typically produces gradually worsening, persistent deficits rather than brief, electric, trigger-induced pain spells. Examination frequently reveals facial asymmetry or sensory changes, and imaging often shows a mass along the facial nerve pathway. The case features normal interictal neurologic findings and stimulus-evoked paroxysms in the trigeminal distribution, which are not explained by geniculate ganglion compression. Choice D: Small-vessel ischemia of brainstem sensory nuclei reflects lacunar pathology from lipohyalinosis or microatheroma in long-standing hypertension or diabetes, producing sustained sensory loss, dysesthesias, or ataxic syndromes depending on the tract involved. Deficits are continuous, often with accompanying objective signs on examination, and onset correlates with an ischemic event rather than dozens of brief attacks per day. Pain from central post-stroke phenomena is typically persistent or waxing–waning, not seconds-long shocks triggered by light touch. A normal neurologic examination between episodes and stimulus sensitivity argue strongly against a lacunar ischemic mechanism. Choice E: Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion classically causes herpes zoster in the V1–V3 dermatomes, presenting with prodromal burning pain followed by a unilateral vesicular eruption on an erythematous base and possible ocular involvement with V1 disease. Pathogenesis involves latent varicella-zoster virus replicating within sensory neurons, leading to inflammation and persistent neuropathic pain that may evolve into postherpetic neuralgia. The clinical course features continuous or lingering pain rather than very brief, electric, triggerable paroxysms; skin findings are typical, and examination often reveals cutaneous lesions. The absence of rash and the paroxysmal, touch- precipitated pattern make zoster reactivation unlikely here. Bottom line: Trigeminal neuralgia most commonly results from microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root by an aberrant arterial loop, producing focal demyelination and ephaptic cross-talk between sensory fibers. This pathology converts normal tactile stimuli into intense paroxysmal pain, often precipitated by light touch or facial movement, and is managed with sodium channel–blocking anticonvulsants. Herpetic neuralgia Choice E: Temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis) is a granulomatous vasculitis affecting large and medium arteries, classically the branches of the external carotid artery in patients over 50 years old. Symptoms include new-onset temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual disturbances (amaurosis fugax), and possible association with polymyalgia rheumatica. Diagnosis is supported by markedly elevated ESR and temporal artery biopsy showing multinucleated giant cells. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids are indicated to prevent irreversible blindness. Temporal arteritis does not present with short, stabbing, periorbital attacks or autonomic symptoms, and is rare in patients under

Trochlear nerve palsy can occur due to increased intracranial pressure or brainstem lesions, but it does not cause ptosis or pupillary changes. Management focuses on addressing underlying causes, such as reducing intracranial pressure or treating infection/tumor. Choice B: The medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF) is a brainstem tract coordinating horizontal eye movements between cranial nerves III and VI. Lesions cause internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), classically presenting as impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye with nystagmus of the abducting contralateral eye during horizontal gaze. INO is commonly seen in multiple sclerosis (younger adults) or brainstem stroke (older adults). The illustration would show failure of one eye to adduct past midline with preserved convergence. No ptosis, mydriasis, or 'down and out' position occurs. Treatment targets the underlying neurologic disorder. Choice D: The optic nerve (cranial nerve II) transmits visual information. Lesions produce monocular vision loss, relative afferent pupillary defect, reduced visual acuity, and possibly central scotoma or loss of color vision (as in optic neuritis). The illustration would show a loss of visual field in one eye, not abnormal eye movement or ptosis. Optic nerve damage occurs in demyelinating diseases (multiple sclerosis), ischemic optic neuropathy, or compressive lesions. Empiric treatment for optic neuritis is high-dose corticosteroids; other causes require disease-specific therapy. Choice E: The trochlear nerve (cranial nerve IV) innervates the superior oblique muscle, which primarily intorts and depresses the eye when adducted. A lesion results in vertical diplopia, worse when looking down and in (e.g., reading, descending stairs). Patients characteristically tilt their head away from the affected side to compensate. The illustration would show an eye slightly elevated when attempting downward gaze and head tilt to the contralateral side. Trochlear nerve palsy rarely produces a blown pupil or ptosis and is most commonly caused by trauma (due to its long intracranial course) or microvascular ischemia. Treatment is generally supportive, with corrective prism glasses or surgery for persistent diplopia. Bottom line: A "down and out" eye with ptosis and mydriasis after head trauma is classic for oculomotor nerve (CN III) palsy, often due to direct injury, aneurysmal compression, or uncal herniation. Transverse inflammation of the thoracic spinal cord Choice A: This would typically result in anterior spinal artery syndrome, which affects the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord. The classic findings include loss of motor function and loss of pain and temperature sensation below the lesion, with preserved dorsal column function (vibration and proprioception). However, this patient has peripheral nerve involvement (decreased reflexes, saddle anesthesia, weak rectal tone), pointing to lower motor neuron injury rather than spinal cord infarction. Furthermore, this condition usually has sudden onset, whereas this patient's presentation is gradual. Choice C: This would be characteristic of conditions like multiple sclerosis (MS), which typically presents in younger patients and often causes central nervous system demyelination, not peripheral nerve root involvement. MS may present with spasticity and upper motor neuron signs, such as increased tone and hyperreflexia. In contrast, this patient has lower motor neuron signs (e.g., decreased reflexes) and a cancer history that makes compression more likely than demyelination. Choice D: Injury to the conus medullaris would also present with bladder dysfunction and perianal numbness, but it tends to occur suddenly and often results in symmetric findings. This patient's gradual onset, asymmetric weakness, and clear lower motor neuron signs with involvement of multiple lumbosacral nerve roots favor cauda equina syndrome instead. In addition, the conus is located at L1–L2, and damage there would typically not cause hyporeflexia of the knees (L3–L4). Choice E: This describes transverse myelitis, which is an inflammatory lesion affecting the spinal cord at a single segment. It commonly presents with bilateral motor and sensory loss, a clearly defined sensory level, and often autonomic dysfunction. However, this patient's findings — including saddle anesthesia, decreased rectal tone, and asymmetric leg symptoms — are not consistent with a thoracic spinal cord lesion, especially given the lack of a sensory level. Bottom line: Cauda equina syndrome = gradual-onset urinary retention + leg pain + saddle anesthesia Caused by compression of lumbosacral nerve roots (LMN signs). Right pons Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Left lateral medulla would produce Wallenberg syndrome, involving CNs IX and X, with hoarseness, dysphagia, and sensory deficits—not tongue deviation or hemiparesis. Choice C: Right lateral medulla would produce symptoms on the opposite side of the brainstem and does not explain leftward tongue deviation or right hemiparesis. Choice D: Right medial medulla would result in right tongue deviation and left-sided hemiparesis, opposite of this patient’s findings. Choice E: The pons contains nuclei for CN V, VI, and VII. Lesions here do not cause tongue deviation, which is a medullary sign. Bottom line: A medial medullary lesion causes ipsilateral tongue deviation and contralateral hemiparesis due to damage to CN XII and corticospinal fibers.
